definition: Higher financing costs structurally reprice physical commodity inventory economics. At SOFR ~4.3%, financing LME copper warehouse stocks costs ~$400/t/year on $9,000/t commodity (~4.4%) vs sub-1% pre-QE. This has compressed contango in base metals curves and reduced incentives for speculative inventory holding.
evidence: SOFR ~4.3% financing cost on LME copper: ~$400/t/year on $9,000/t commodity
Compressed contango observed in base metals forward curves
Reduced incentives for speculative inventory holding
Arithmetic is straightforward — the transmission mechanism is direct
Attribution of LME stock declines to financing costs vs physical tightness is unclear
open_questions: What fraction of LME stock declines is attributable to financing costs vs physical market tightness?
How does the financing cost pass through to end-user commodity prices?
At what SOFR level would inventory economics revert to pre-QE dynamics?
Does this effect net reduce or increase commodity price volatility?
